data.title: Esquire Financial Holdings: Avoided A Downgrade With A Transformative Acquisition
data.description:
March 12th was a very interesting day for shareholders of Esquire Financial Holdings, Inc. (ESQ), a rather interesting bank with a market capitalization today of about $803 million. Shares of the company jumped 7% after management announced a fascinating acquisition. In the past, I have been rather neutral on the business. In fact, in late December of 2025, I reaffirmed it as a Hold candidate. This was based on the fact that, while asset quality was fantastic, shares of the business looked expensive. I even concluded that the stock was becoming difficult to justify at its valuation back then and that a downgrade was becoming increasingly likely. Since that time, the stock has inched higher by only 0.1%, which is better than the 3.7% drop that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. But this outperformance is only because of the acquisition-fueled increase that the company exhibited on March 12th.

Looking at the transaction in question, I actually do feel optimistic. Because we don't really know the full impact of the deal until after it is completed and management has an opportunity to cut costs, I won't go so far as to say that upgrading the stock to a Buy is the right choice here. But I do think a case could be made that this significantly reduces the probability of a downgrade. Based on this, I feel very comfortable maintaining Esquire Financial Holdings as a Hold at this moment.

A value-accretive purchase

Before we get into the terms of the transaction, I think it would be helpful to touch on the overall financial health of Esquire Financial Holdings. In the chart above, you can see certain balance sheet data, covering deposits, loans, securities, cash, and debt. Most notably, at the end of 2025, the company had deposits of $2.06 billion. That was up significantly over the $1.64 billion that the company had in 2024. As deposits increased, the value of loans grew also. They expanded from $1.38 billion to $1.73 billion. This is typical when you consider how fast deposits are rising. After all, deposits are literal fuel for growth when it comes to financial institutions like banks.

Naturally, there are other aspects of the balance sheet that we should not ignore. Securities came in at $309.9 million in 2025. That was down ever so slightly from the $313.4 million that the company had a year earlier. Cash and cash equivalents jumped from $126.3 million to $235.9 million. And debt, meanwhile, remain flat at a big old goose egg.

Overall, I'm very happy with this structure. The only real complaint that I have when it comes to the balance sheet is the fact that uninsured deposit exposure is a bit higher than I would like it to be. Total deposits that were uninsured ended up being $685.1 million. That translates to approximately 33.2% of all deposits. Typically, I like this number to be 30% or lower. This is not a drastic disparity, but it is a disparity all the same.

One of the big differentiators when it comes to Esquire Financial Holdings is the fact that its business is primarily focused on providing services to those in the legal industry. The litigation space is a niche market. And by focusing on this niche, the company has the ability to grow rapidly while also extracting significant value from its clients. You can only imagine how valuable a bank that specializes in litigation is to those enterprises that operate within that market. So the amount of value capture that exists here is meaningful.

To illustrate this, we need only look at the chart above and the chart below. Above, you can see income statement data for the last three completed fiscal years. The overall rise in the size of the company's balance sheet pushed net interest income up from $79.2 million in 2023 to $111.8 million in 2025. One thing that certainly helped here was the fact that, at a time when many banks were dealing with margin compression because of needing to compete with other institutions in a high interest rate environment, Esquire Financial Holdings saw its overall net interest margin remained virtually flat. It was 6.09% in 2023. It amounted to 6.06% in 2024. And in 2025, it was only slightly lower at 6.02%. This is virtually indistinguishable from one year to the next. And the margins generated by the company drastically exceed what is typical in the banking sector. Usually, banks have net interest margins of between 2% and 3%. Every so often, you might see something approaching 4%. But 6% on a sustained basis is very rare indeed.

The company is, at its core, a high quality player. That is why I have struggled with it in the past in terms of valuation. Having said that, I do think my overall track record was pretty good. In fact, from the time I had first became bullish on it in June of 2024 until I subsequently downgraded it to a ‘hold’ in September of last year, the stock jumped 114.1%. That was drastically higher than the 19.8% rise that the market saw over the same window of time. The problem was not quality. Rather, it's that valuation became challenging for the business. And as I mentioned at the start of this article, I was very close to downgrading the company when I last wrote about it.

To illustrate for you just how expensive shares are, I need only point you to the chart above. In it, you can see that the business is trading at a price to earnings multiple of 15.8. That is well above the 10 maximum that I typically like to see. The chart also shows its price to book multiple and price to tangible book multiple. I typically like this number to come in closer to 1. Of course, a case could be made, and I did make it in the past, that high quality justified a premium. In the chart below, you can see a return on assets reading of 2.36% for the bank. This is drastically higher than the 1% minimum I like to see. The chart also shows a return on equity reading of 18.90%. For context, I'd like to see this number come in at 10% or greater. Still, shares were expensive. And, in my last article, I heavily considered a downgrade for the stock.

Management's decision to acquire Signature Bancorporation resolves that matter as far as I am concerned. For context, on March 12th, management announced that they were essentially merging Esquire Financial Holdings with Signature in an all stock deal valued at $348.8 million. Shareholders of the privately held Signature will receive 2.63 shares of Esquire Financial Holdings for each share of Signature that they currently own. However, this transaction could change a bit based on the disposition value of some of the loans that are on Signature’s books. These loans have a par value today of $70 million. So the ultimate exchange ratio will be adjusted based on the proceeds of these loans, with a ratio no lower than 2.50 and no higher than 2.80. At the low end, this should mean a total value of $331.2 million. And at the high end, we are looking at a value of about $370.9 million.

Conceptually, Esquire Financial Holdings’ decision to acquire Signature makes a lot of sense to me. Management has positioned this as a strategically attractive purchase, specifically because the acquisition will reduce its litigation vertical loan and funding concentrations from more than 70% to less than 50%. For those not familiar, Signature specializes in serving privately held, middle market companies that benefit from its high touch commercial banking model.

Over its lifetime, from 2006 until the present day, Signature has expanded from a very small institution to one boasting assets of $2 billion. Total loans held for investment amount to roughly $1.3 billion. One thing that investors in Esquire Financial Holdings might not like about the institution is that its net interest margin is lower at 4.13%. But as I mentioned earlier in this article, that is still a very attractive net interest margin. Return on assets are also lower at 1.85%. However, Esquire Financial Holdings is not acquiring Signature for quality alone. It is acquiring it for value.

Depending on the aforementioned loans that will be sold off, the effective price to earnings multiple being paid for Signature will range between 8.6 and 9.6. This is below the 10 maximum that I typically like to see and will serve to reduce Esquire Financial Holdings’ effective price to earnings multiple. In fact, management at Esquire Financial Holdings believes that, at the midpoint of guidance, this acquisition will boost earnings per share by 23% by 2027. At current pricing for Esquire Financial Holdings, that reduces its price to earnings multiple from 15.8 to 14.7. The purchase should also increase its tangible book value by between 8% and 15% at the time that the transaction closes. That would reduce its price to tangible book multiple from 3.14 to about 2.82. That's still high, but it's not as high as it was before.

Another benefit behind this transaction is that, in addition to making Esquire Financial Holdings cheaper and more operationally diverse, it also expands the company into Chicago, with its metropolitan statistical area serving as the third most populous area in the U.S. There's about 9.4 million people there, and it is responsible for about $886 billion of GDP for the U.S. It's currently ranked as the number one US metro for corporate relocation and expansion. And total deposits in the area amount to $601 billion.

Median household income in Chicago is about $97,107 per year. That is 11.8% above the $86,867 that we see nationally in the US. What's more, that trend should continue to grow. Between 2026 and 2031, it's believed that household income will grow 11.3%. That is specifically for the U.S. But for Chicago, that number is expected to be 13.1%. Even though this is a bid at diversification, there is also the angle of expanding further into the litigation vertical here. In fact, Chicago is the 4th largest law firm market in the U.S. and the third largest when it comes to contingency fee law firms. The company already has a 0.9% share of the litigation market in Chicago. But that's well below the 1.9% in Houston and the 6.9% in New York. At the low end, management expects its share of the market in Chicago to jump to 1.9%. And if everything goes great, that number could increase further to 6.9%.

There will be some other developments here than investors need to be aware of. For instance, management expects that cost savings will be $2.1 million on a full run rate basis by 2027. That works out to about 5% of Signature’s non-interest expense. All projections provided by the company do not include revenue synergies that have been identified by the business. So there is the possibility that the end result of this transaction would be even more favorable than what management has indicated. But there will be certain costs as part of this entire process. This includes $35.5 million of pre-tax merger expenses. It also involves $49.5 million of certain projected gross loans, pre-tax credit marks, certain interest rate hits, including a $56.8 million write down of available for sale securities, and other factors. But on the whole, this transaction still looks appealing in my view.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I would argue that the purchase of Signature by Esquire Financial Holdings is a positive one for shareholders. It diversifies the company. It uses more expensive stock to acquire a cheaper business. It opens up the company to new opportunities, both on the litigation side and outside of it. And it significantly improves bottom line results and its valuation. This is not enough for me to upgrade Esquire Financial Holdings at this time. But it is enough for me to maintain ESQ as a Hold candidate.

data.title: Boeing: New 737 MAX Problem, Stock Down Despite Solid Delivery Performance
data.description:
Boeing (NYSE:BA) stock has lost 6.7% of its value since my last report underperforming the S&P 500’s 1.4% decline. Boeing reported its orders and deliveries February, which showed strength but amid a broader pressure for industrial stocks the stock price declined. Boeing also encountered a wiring issue on the Boeing 737 MAX, which will impact Q1 delivery numbers. In this report, I discuss Boeing’s February airplane orders and deliveries.

Boeing Adds Tanker And Dreamliner Orders

For this report, I'm using a tool developed by The Aerospace Forum to visualize airplane orders, deliveries, cancellations, and associated values.

In February, Boeing booked 21 orders compared to 107 orders booked in January. The order inflow consisted of seven single-aisle airplanes and 14 wide-body airplanes including eight freighters with a combined value of $2.4 billion:

  • Air Astana ordered five Boeing 787-9s.
  • Two unidentified customers ordered a total of three Boeing 787-9 airplanes.
  • An unidentified customer ordered seven Boeing 737 MAX airplanes.
  • An unidentified customer ordered six Boeing 767-2C airplanes.

During the month, the following changes were made to the order book:

  • Westjet cancelled orders for six Boeing 737 MAX airplanes.
  • Vietnam Airlines was identified as the customer for 60 Boeing 737 MAX airplanes.
  • Shenzhen Airlines was identified as the customer for one Boeing 737 MAX.
  • Southwest Airlines was identified as the customer for two Boeing 737 MAX airplanes.
  • Qatar Airways was identified as the customer for one Boeing 777F.
  • An unidentified customer selected GE Aerospace (GE) as the engine supplier for one Boeing 787-8 on order.
  • Etihad Airways selected GE Aerospace as the engine supplier for six Boeing 787-10s.
  • Hawaiian Airlines (ALK) selected GE Aerospace as the engine supplier for three Boeing 787-10s.
  • United Airlines (UAL) selected GE Aerospace as the engine supplier for 33 Boeing 787-10s and 17 Boeing 787-9s.
  • Ethiopian Airlines selected GE Aerospace as the engine supplier for three Boeing 787-9s.
  • Flydubai selected GE Aerospace as the engine supplier for 30 Boeing 787-9s.
  • An unidentified customer selected GE Aerospace (GE) as the engine supplier for one Boeing 787-9 on order.
  • Sun Phu Quoc Airways was identified as the customer for 20 Boeing 787-9s.

February was not a huge month for Boeing with just 21 orders, but we note that the Boeing 787 and Boeing 737 continued to gather new orders. Boeing booked 15 net orders valued $2.1 billion. What was perhaps more noteworthy is that we saw a significant number of customers selecting the GE aero engines for their Boeing 787 orders. That is not a huge surprise. In a prior report on GE Aerospace and Rolls-Royce (RYCEF), I already noted that GE is leading over RR for the Boeing 787 platform.

In the same month last year, Boeing booked five net orders valued $54.3 million. So, year-on-year net orders and net order value increased driven by a higher gross orders and a more attractive gross order mix while cancellations were higher this year, but last year’s cancellations also included wide body order cancellations. Year-to-date, Boeing has booked 118 valued at $10.9 billion compared to 41 net orders valued at $2.1 billion a year ago.

ASC 606 adjustments increased by nine units for the Boeing 737 program. ASC 606 adjustments might be a vague term. Therefore, I have provided a description from prior analyses about what they are:

ASC 606 adjustments are corrections to the backlog for orders where a purchase contract exists, but additional criteria are not met. A decrease in ASC 606 adjustments can be achieved when the order, in fact, does get cancelled or the additional criteria are met.

Boeing currently has a total of 590 orders in the ASC 606 adjustment category, which covers 8.8% of the backlog, up from 8.6% in the prior month. As previously stated, it does not mean that 8.8% of the backlog is necessarily prone to cancellation, but for 8.8% of the backlog, additional criteria are not yet met. Occasionally, this leads to cancellations.

Boeing Airplane Deliveries Show Little Growth

In February, Boeing delivered 51 airplanes, consisting of 43 single-aisle airplanes and eight wide-body airplanes valued at $3.3 billion:

  • Boeing delivered 43 Boeing 737 MAX airplanes.
  • Boeing delivered one Boeing 767-2C, which is the base configuration for the KC-46A tanker, and two Boeing 767-300F airplanes.
  • Boeing delivered two 777F airplanes.
  • Boeing delivered three Boeing 787-9s.

Sequentially, Boeing’s delivery numbers increased from 46 to 51. This was driven by 737 MAX deliveries increasing by six units, an increase of one unit on the Boeing 767 program partially offset by lower Boeing 777 and Boeing 787 deliveries. Due to a less attractive mix, delivery numbers increased but the value remained stable at $3.3 billion. Year-to-date, Boeing has delivered 97 airplanes valued $6.6 billion compared to 89 deliveries valued at $6.2 billion a year ago. The increase was driven by the Boeing 737 program. Boeing is now fully reliant on the production rates to increase production. In that light, the new wiring issue is on the Boeing 737 MAX program is a setback. However, the expectation is that while Q1 deliveries will be impacted there will not be any full-year impact. In some way, the system is working the way it should as the issue was detected. However, at this point I also believe that Boeing shouldn’t be having these issues at all.

The book-to-bill ratio, an indicator of supply-demand health, was 0.4x in terms of orders and 0.7x in terms of value. For the first two months of the year, these ratios were 1.3x and 1.7x reflecting continued healthy order inflow with upward pressure on production from demand perspective.

Conclusion: Middle East Turmoil And 737 MAX Issue Do Not Affect Boeing’s Value Proposition

The turmoil in the Middle East does have a negative impact on sentiment for industrial stocks. Naturally, one would argue that high oil prices would boost demand for new fuel-efficient airplanes. However, with backlogs stretching years, the bigger concern has shifted towards any impact on the macroeconomic impact of high oil prices and global unrest. The reality is that high oil prices are good for jet makers, but oil prices being too high is also not helpful. The Boeing 737 MAX problems are somewhat disappointing, but they currently are not expected to affect the full year performance. I remain bullish on Boeing as I do believe that the market is resilient and names like Boeing are attractive for the longer term.

data.title: Private Credit In A New Refinancing Environment
data.description:
Recent market commentary has highlighted liquidity pressures, particularly rising redemption requests across several large business development companies (BDCs) and other semi-liquid vehicles, prompting increased scrutiny of the asset class.

The broader reality, however, is more nuanced. Private credit is entering a new phase of the cycle shaped by refinancing dynamics, liquidity design, and widening dispersion across managers and strategies. In this environment, outcomes are likely to reward disciplined underwriting, resilient portfolio construction and managers capable of navigating a more difficult refinancing world.

The next credit cycle

Private credit has always been cyclical. What distinguishes this phase is the starting point. Pandemic-era rates allowed companies to borrow at exceptionally low costs, often with more aggressive capital structures. Some were refinanced amid continued strong demand for private credit, extending maturities. Today, inflation and higher base rates have reset the environment, and many of these organizations that had been hoping the rate environment would revert need to refinance again under materially different conditions.

A meaningful portion of some portfolios was built in 2021 and 2022, when capital was inexpensive and valuation multiples were elevated, as significant inflows into retail funds required immediate deployment. That context matters as those vintages approach refinancing in a higher-rate regime.

From optional to necessary refinancing

Private credit that was not refinanced is now doing so in a materially higher rate environment, with floating-rate coupons having already reduced free cash flow. Because maturities typically fall five to seven years from origination – and sponsors rarely wait until the final year to refinance – refinancing shifts from optional to necessary, and at a time when capital is becoming scarcer due to fund flows.

Even against this backdrop, corporate fundamentals remain constructive in aggregate, and income generation remains attractive. In more challenging credit environments, defaults typically affect a minority of borrowers. Uncertainty around which borrowers may face stress can materially tighten credit conditions and widen spreads as capital flows slow.

Lower-quality, highly levered borrowers with near-term maturities are likely to feel pressure first. As prevailing yields make refinancing more expensive – and in some cases uneconomic – amendment activity and liability management are increasing. Some transactions may resemble refinancings that delay immediate pressure but, in practice, reflect complex intercreditor dynamics as lenders seek to protect their positions in a potential restructuring or bankruptcy filing.

This dynamic can represent a structural advantage of private lending, as defaults may be addressed over time rather than through simultaneous liquidations during a broad downturn, typically the most challenging environment for recoveries. Instead of a sharp dislocation, the adjustment is more likely to unfold as a gradual reset of capital structures: negotiated restructurings, maturity extensions, repricing of risk, and incremental equity support.

As refinancing shifts from optional to necessary, more sponsors may reassess the prospects for a profitable exit on certain deals originated before the move to significantly higher rates. That is why we have typically favored GPs with disciplined underwriting and operational expertise; helping businesses recover and maximize outcomes during difficult periods is when quality private credit managers truly separate themselves.

Questioning headline hype

Recent coverage of private credit has been largely negative, particularly around liquidity concerns. However, while those issues deserve scrutiny, most have limited relevance for the majority of investors.

On stock performance, many publicly traded private credit managers have seen their share prices decline meaningfully. That reflects a re-rating of asset management businesses following a period of rapid growth and elevated margins, rather than an automatic read-through to underlying portfolio performance.

Similarly, liquidity headlines are more nuanced and often center on redemption activity in semi-liquid vehicles. Many investors were drawn to private credit with what felt like a promise of liquidity, and that liquidity is now being tested. Most open-end funds disclose quarterly redemption limits, yet it can still come as a surprise when those limits are reached. While gating may be disappointing, it does not automatically imply credit impairment.

The more consequential issue is how credit managers respond. Redemption limits are designed to protect investors from the structural mismatch between private assets and periodic withdrawals at net asset value (NAV). Accommodating redemptions beyond required thresholds may provide short-term flexibility, but it can introduce longer-term portfolio consequences.

Selling assets to meet redemptions is where those trade-offs become more tangible. Private credit’s structural advantage has long been single-lender control, which can simplify negotiations in stressed situations. Selling down positions or clubbing transactions may dilute that control. Selling materially below valuation marks can crystallize losses and reshape portfolio composition. Even transactions executed near marks may alter the concentration and risk profile of what remains.

What will differentiate

Real market stress reveals differences in credit selection and portfolio construction. When capital is easy, those differences are less visible. When refinancing is no longer automatic, outcomes are more likely to diverge. We see three key areas that differentiate private credit managers in the current environment.

Real market stress reveals differences in credit selection and portfolio construction. When capital is easy, those differences are less visible. When refinancing is no longer automatic, outcomes are more likely to diverge. We see three key areas that differentiate private credit managers in the current environment.

1. Selectivity

Strong businesses with durable cash flow and engaged sponsor support should remain relatively resilient. Over-levered credits are more likely to face restructuring pressure and increased liability management.

2. Deliberate portfolio construction

Diversification must be deliberate and focused on sources of cash flow and types of collateral – not simply the number of positions. A portfolio can hold many loans and still be exposed to the same economic driver. For example, a portfolio of 500 loans, 40% of which are U.S. private equity-owned software companies, is not meaningfully diversified. True diversification comes from exposure across distinct economic drivers, owners, collateral and capital structures, and providing flexibility when individual credits face stress.

3. Liquidity management

Liquidity management is increasingly central at this stage of the cycle. Operating close to leverage limits with minimal buffer may appear efficient in calm markets; however, it leaves limited room to maneuver when refinancing pressure builds or investor sentiment shifts. A modest sacrifice in yield, or the cost of maintaining unused line capacity, can be a reasonable price to preserve portfolio integrity without altering portfolio construction at the most inopportune time.

Investor implications

In a new refinancing environment, separating headline noise from structural change will be critical as access to private credit continues to expand across investor channels. Private credit is inherently illiquid, regardless of vehicle structure, and the return premium associated with the asset class reflects a long-term commitment of capital rather than tactical trading.

Private credit will likely continue to generate headlines as markets move through this phase of the cycle. Refinancing activity, liquidity design, and widening performance dispersion will shape the landscape across managers and strategies. In this environment, underwriting discipline, thoughtful portfolio construction and prudent liquidity management are likely to differentiate outcomes. For investors, separating structural realities from headline noise will remain essential.

These views are subject to change at any time based upon market or other conditions and are current as of the date at the top of the page. The information, analysis, and opinions expressed herein are for general information only and are not intended to provide specific advice or recommendations for any individual or entity.

This material is not an offer, solicitation or recommendation to purchase any security.

Forecasting represents predictions of market prices and/or volume patterns utilizing varying analytical data. It is not representative of a projection of the stock market, or of any specific investment.

Nothing contained in this material is intended to constitute legal, tax, securities or investment advice, nor an opinion regarding the appropriateness of any investment. The general information contained in this publication should not be acted upon without obtaining specific legal, tax and investment advice from a licensed professional.

Diversification and strategic asset allocation do not assure a profit or guarantee against loss in declining markets.

Please remember that all investments carry some level of risk, including the potential loss of principal invested. They do not typically grow at an even rate of return and may experience negative growth. As with any type of portfolio structuring, attempting to reduce risk and increase return could, at certain times, unintentionally reduce returns.

The Russell Investments logo is a trademark and service mark of Russell Investments

The information, analyses and opinions set forth herein are intended to serve as general information only and should not be relied upon by any individual or entity as advice or recommendations specific to that individual entity. Anyone using this material should consult with their own attorney, accountant, financial or tax adviser or consultants on whom they rely for investment advice specific to their own circumstances.

Products and services described on this website are intended for United States residents only. Nothing contained in this material is intended to constitute legal, tax, securities, or investment advice, nor an opinion regarding the appropriateness of any investment, nor a solicitation of any type. The general information contained on this website should not be acted upon without obtaining specific legal, tax, and investment advice from a licensed professional. Persons outside the United States may find more information about products and services available within their jurisdictions by going to Russell Investments' Worldwide site.

Russell Investments is committed to ensuring digital accessibility for people with disabilities. We are continually improving the user experience for everyone, and applying the relevant accessibility standards.

Russell Investments' ownership is composed of a majority stake held by funds managed by TA Associates Management, L.P., with a significant minority stake held by funds managed by Reverence Capital Partners, L.P. Certain of Russell Investments' employees and Hamilton Lane Advisors, LLC also hold minority, non-controlling, ownership stakes.

Frank Russell Company is the owner of the Russell trademarks contained in this material and all trademark rights related to the Russell trademarks, which the members of the Russell Investments group of companies are permitted to use under license from Frank Russell Company. The members of the Russell Investments group of companies are not affiliated in any manner with Frank Russell Company or any entity operating under the "FTSE RUSSELL" brand.
